title: HSE Guidance: Display Screen Equipment (DSE) — Working with Display Screen Equipment
chapters: HSE guidance L26 · Health and Safety (Display Screen Equipment) Regulations 1992 (SI 1992/2792) as amended 2002 · Applies to all users and operators of DSE · Covers workstation assessment, daily work routine, eye tests, training, and homeworking
desc: The HSE guidance on Display Screen Equipment covers the employer's obligations under the Health and Safety (Display Screen Equipment) Regulations 1992 (as amended 2002). The Regulations apply to employees who habitually use DSE as a significant part of their normal work (defined as 'users'). Employers must: carry out a workstation assessment for each DSE user (reg.2), ensure workstations meet minimum requirements (Schedule to the Regulations), plan daily work routine to include breaks or changes of activity (reg.4), provide eye and eyesight tests on request and, if the test shows that special corrective appliances are needed specifically for DSE work, provide them (reg.5), and provide adequate health and safety training for DSE users (reg.6). The Regulations are particularly relevant in the context of homeworking and hybrid working — employer obligations extend to home workstations where employees habitually use DSE. The guidance was updated to address homeworking following the COVID-19 pandemic. For FM sector employers like the Northerly Hill demo entity, DSE obligations apply primarily to office-based and administrative staff, not to operatives performing manual FM duties.
legalStatus: HSE guidance supporting the Health and Safety (Display Screen Equipment) Regulations 1992. The Regulations are legally binding; the guidance provides practical compliance methodology.

sections.title: Workstation assessment, breaks, eye tests, and training
sections.summary: Regulation 2 requires employers to carry out a suitable and sufficient assessment of DSE workstations to assess the health and safety risks. The assessment must consider: the display screen (size, image quality, brightness, contrast, tilt, swivel), the keyboard (tiltable, separate from screen, space in front, matt surface), work desk/surface (sufficiently large, low reflectance, document holder if needed), work chair (adjustable height and back, footrest if needed), space (sufficient to allow changes of position), lighting (appropriate, no glare or reflections on screen), reflections and glare (workstation positioned to avoid reflections), noise (equipment noise should not distract), heat (equipment should not produce excessive heat), radiation (reduced to negligible levels), and humidity (adequate levels maintained). Regulation 4 requires employers to plan activities so that daily work on DSE is periodically interrupted by breaks or changes of activity that reduce the workload at the screen. Regulation 5 requires employers to provide eye and eyesight tests on request, and if the test identifies a need for special corrective appliances specifically for DSE work, to provide them at the employer's cost. Regulation 6 requires adequate health and safety training for DSE users on the risks and the measures taken to reduce them.
sections.text:
Key obligations under the DSE Regulations 1992:

Workstation assessment (reg.2):
• Assess all workstations used by DSE 'users' (employees who habitually use DSE as a significant part of normal work)
• Assessment must consider all elements of the workstation: screen, keyboard, desk, chair, environment
• Identify risks and reduce them to the lowest extent reasonably practicable
• Review assessments when there is a significant change (new equipment, change in work patterns, change in workstation)

Breaks and changes of activity (reg.4):
• Daily work on DSE must be periodically interrupted by breaks or changes of activity
• Short, frequent breaks are more effective than longer, infrequent breaks
• Breaks should be taken before the onset of fatigue, not to recover from it
• The content and timing of breaks should allow for individual differences

Eye tests (reg.5):
• Employers must provide eye and eyesight tests for DSE users on request
• If the test identifies a need for special corrective appliances specifically for DSE work (as opposed to general use), the employer must provide them at no cost to the employee
• Tests should be available before the employee starts DSE work, at regular intervals thereafter, and if the employee experiences visual difficulties

Training (reg.6):
• Adequate training for all DSE users on the risks associated with DSE use and the measures taken to reduce them
• Training should cover correct workstation setup, the importance of breaks, how to adjust equipment, and how to report problems

Homeworking:
• The Regulations apply to home workstations where the employee habitually uses DSE
• Employers should carry out a workstation assessment for home workers, which may be done by self-assessment with guidance
• Employers should provide, or contribute to, suitable equipment for the home workstation

versions.description: Original Health and Safety (Display Screen Equipment) Regulations 1992 (SI 1992/2792) came into force, implementing EU Directive 90/270/EEC on minimum safety and health requirements for work with display screen equipment.
Health and Safety (Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations 2002 (SI 2002/2174) amended the DSE Regulations to extend coverage and update requirements.

leadingCases.name: Munkenbeck & Marshall v Michael Harold [1995] (Unreported, IT)
leadingCases.heldText: An early tribunal case where an employee's employer was found liable for upper limb disorder (repetitive strain injury) caused by prolonged DSE use without adequate breaks or workstation assessment. The employer had failed to carry out any risk assessment under the DSE Regulations.
leadingCases.significance: Early authority establishing employer liability for musculoskeletal injury caused by inadequate DSE provision.

leadingCases.name: Pickford v Imperial Chemical Industries plc [1998] 1 WLR 1189
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords considered a claim for repetitive strain injury (RSI) from typing. While the claim failed on causation, the case confirmed that employers owe a duty of care in respect of DSE-related musculoskeletal disorders, and that the DSE Regulations are relevant to defining the standard of care.
leadingCases.significance: House of Lords authority confirming employer duty of care for DSE-related injury. The DSE Regulations inform the standard of care expected.

refs: Health and Safety (Display Screen Equipment) Regulations 1992 (SI 1992/2792)
HSWA 1974 s.2 (employer's general duty — DSE risks fall within scope)
MHSWR 1999 reg.3 (general risk assessment — DSE assessment is a specific application)
EU Directive 90/270/EEC (minimum requirements for DSE work)
HSE L26: Work with Display Screen Equipment

pendingAmendments: Post-Brexit — DSE Regs now assimilated law
HSE updated guidance on homeworking DSE assessments post-COVID — monitoring for further updates

leadingCases.name: Pickford v ICI plc [1998] 1 WLR 1189
leadingCases.heldText: Employers owe a duty of care for DSE-related musculoskeletal disorders. The DSE Regulations inform the standard of care.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on employer liability for DSE-related injury.

leadingCases.name: Alexander v Midland Bank plc [1999] EWCA Civ 1918
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal considered a work-related upper limb disorder claim arising from keyboard use. The case examined the adequacy of the employer's risk assessment and workstation arrangements.
leadingCases.significance: Applied the DSE Regulations framework to assess employer's compliance with the duty of care for keyboard-intensive work.

parliamentaryOrigin.enablingAct: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 s.15 (regulations power)
parliamentaryOrigin.euDirective: Council Directive 90/270/EEC on minimum safety and health requirements for work with display screen equipment
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The DSE Regulations implement EU Directive 90/270/EEC. As regulatory guidance, the HSE L26 document provides practical compliance methodology but is not itself legally binding — the Regulations are the legal obligation.
